coredata.dc:title: Effect of lithium salts LiPF6 and LiBF4 on combustion properties of electrolyte with EC/PC/EMC under different pressures
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Shibiao
Meng, Xiaokai
Cao, Weiguo
Yu, Shuo
Liu, Changcheng
Huang, Que
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the combustion properties of ethylene carbonate/propylene carbonate/ethyl methyl carbonate (EC/PC/EMC) with LiPF6 or LiPF4 were studied by cone calorimeter under various environmental pressures in Hefei (100.09 kPa), Lijiang (76.11 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa), respectively. The impact of lithium salts and pressure on the significant fire parameters of electrolyte such as flame temperature, mass loss rate (MLR) and heat release rate (HRR) were analyzed to evaluate the degree of thermal hazard. The experimental results showed that with the decrease of ambient pressure, the peak HRR (pHRR) and total heat release (THR) declined, and the combustion duration increased significantly. In the steady combustion stage of electrolyte pool fire, the mean value of MLR had an exponential relationship with the external pressure. The error of HRR obtained by oxygen consumption (OC) calorimetry and stoichiometric thermochemistry (TC) theory was about 15%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrolyte
Pressure
Lithium-ion battery
Heat release rate
Mass loss rate
Flame temperature

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of combustion experimental platform in Hefei (100.09 kPa), including a cone calorimeter, and a thermocouple to detect the flame temperature.
Positions of thermocouples in Lijiang (76.11 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa).
Combustion phenomena and four stages of electrolyte pool fire.

(a) The average center line flame temperatures measured by thermocouples at various positions under different pressures of Lijiang (76.11 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa), and (b) the average flame temperature at T1 under various pressures of Hefei (100.09 kPa), Lijiang (76.11 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa), during stable combustion stages of the two electrolytes.
Flame center line temperature at different pressures on the basis of McCaffrey model.
Mass/MLR versus time variation curves of the electrolyte with (a) LiPF6 and (b) LiBF4 in Hefei (100.09 kPa), Lijiang (76.11 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa).
Fitting diagram of average MLR versus pressure.
HRR curves and total heat release (THR) of electrolyte with (a) LiPF6 and (b) LiBF4 in Hefei (100.09 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa).
The HRR curves of the electrolyte with (a) LiPF6 and (b) LiBF4 calculated by two methods: OC calorimetry and TC theory.
Measured CO and CO2 production of electrolyte in Hefei (100.09 kPa).
Carbonate solvents used for thermal safety tests in this study.
Composition and parameters of the two kinds of electrolytes in this paper.

row.entry: 1 mol/L LiPF6 dissolved in EC/PC/EMC (25:25:50, wt%)
1 mol/L LiBF4 dissolved in EC/PC/EMC (25:25:50, wt%)

head.ce:title: Effect of lithium salts LiPF6 and LiBF4 on combustion properties of electrolyte with EC/PC/EMC under different pressures

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the combustion properties of ethylene carbonate/propylene carbonate/ethyl methyl carbonate (EC/PC/EMC) with LiPF6 or LiPF4 were studied by cone calorimeter under various environmental pressures in Hefei (100.09 kPa), Lijiang (76.11 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa), respectively. The impact of lithium salts and pressure on the significant fire parameters of electrolyte such as flame temperature, mass loss rate (MLR) and heat release rate (HRR) were analyzed to evaluate the degree of thermal hazard. The experimental results showed that with the decrease of ambient pressure, the peak HRR (pHRR) and total heat release (THR) declined, and the combustion duration increased significantly. In the steady combustion stage of electrolyte pool fire, the mean value of MLR had an exponential relationship with the external pressure. The error of HRR obtained by oxygen consumption (OC) calorimetry and stoichiometric thermochemistry (TC) theory was about 15%.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion battery (LIB) has the advantages of relatively long service life and high energy density, which is widely used in portable devices, electric vehicles (EVs) and large energy storage stations [1]. With the transformation of energy structure and the updating/upgrading of large-scale electrical equipment, LIB has undoubtedly brought great changes and convenience to people's lives. However, at the same time, the safety problem always exists and has been the most critical obstacle for its future application [2,3]. The typical combustible components in LIB consist mainly of electrolytes (including lithium salts) and separators. The early melting of the separator would lead to a short circuit inside the LIB, generating a large amount of heat instantaneously [4,5]. During thermal runaway (TR) process, LIB would experience complex reactions such as thermal decomposition of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) [6,7], reaction of interlayer lithium with electrolyte [8], pyrolysis of electrolyte [9,10], thermolysis of active material in positive electrode [11], direct reaction of cathode with electrolyte [12], which would release a lot of heat, causing sparks and even explosions [13]. Since LIB could be regarded as a very complicated system, and there are a variety of combustible substances in the battery. Therefore, if the whole LIB is taken as the research object, the numerous intricate chemical reactions are hardly distinguished to comprehensively understand the heat destruction mechanism, and it is difficult to obtain quantitative experimental data that conducive to scientific research. Thus, in the first place, it is necessary to study the combustion behavior of several key combustibles in LIB, especially electrolyte, since whose combustion could release a vast majority of the heat accounting for the entire LIB fire [14]. In addition, the safety of LIB in special environmental conditions, for example, the low-pressure environment for aviation and plateau, has gradually attracted attention [15,16]. Hence, the combustion characteristics of electrolyte under different pressures were studied in this paper. Hefei City (100.09 kPa) in Anhui Province, Lijiang City (76.11 kPa) in Yunan Province and Lhasa City (65.23 kPa) in Tibet Province of China were chosen as the experimental locations.

At present, there are many kinds of electrolytes used in the LIB market. In general, electrolyte is mixed with about 12% lithium salt, 83% solvent and 5% additives (mass ratio). Recently, LiPF6, LiPF4, LiAsF6, LiClO4, LiCF3SO3, LiBC4O8/LiBOB are the most commonly used lithium salts [17–24]. Among them, comprehensively considering conductivity, cost, safety and environmental factors, LiPF6 is one of the most commercially available electrolytes. Besides, LiPF4 with good chemical and thermal stability, which is also sensitive to environmental moisture, and has the ability to develop into the excellent electrolyte system widely applied in energy storage and power LIB.

Secondly, as for the solvent, it is mainly used to ensure the dissolution of lithium salt and ion conduction, which had better have a large enough dielectric constant, in order to reduce the strong electrostatic attraction between the positive and negative electrodes. As the superior electrolyte solvent, which needs to meet the requirement of low melting point, high boiling temperature, wide operating temperature range, and the relatively large dielectric constant as well as the high conductivity. The solvent components mainly include ethyl carbonate (EC), propylene carbonate (PC), methyl ethyl carbonate (EMC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), diethyl carbonate (DEC), etc [25–30]. However, the properties of different solvents conflict with each other. EC and PC have high boiling point and large dielectric constant. In addition, EC would hardly be decomposed on the surface of highly graphitized carbon materials, and could even promote film formation. While both EC and PC have high viscosity. Although DMC has low viscosity, it also has low boiling point. Therefore, mixed solvents are required to compensate for the shortcomings of each component. Generally, solvents with high relative dielectric constant and high viscosity like EC and PC are suitable to be mixed with carbonates with low relative dielectric constant and low viscosity, such as DMC and DEC. Furthermore, EMC has both methyl and ethyl functional groups in its molecular structure, and presents the properties like DMC and DEC. Besides, because of its low viscosity and wide liquid range, the low temperature performance of EMC is outstanding, which is suitable for electrolyte on almost all occasions. As a result, EMC has become one of the largest solvents used at present, and fortunately it could be an alternative for the fixed DMC and DEC. As a consequence, in this paper, EC/PC/EMC mixtures with LiPF6 or LiPF4 were used as experimental samples to study the fire risk of LIB electrolyte.

As for the evaluation of electrolyte fire risk, Zhang et al. [17] studied the combustion behavior of carbonate electrolyte and compared two types of heat release rate (HRR) calculation methods. For the three carbonate solvent mixtures 1.0 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC (1:1, wt/wt), EC/DMC (1:1, wt/wt), and EC/DEC/DMC (1:1:1, wt/wt), respectively, the HRR values measured by oxygen consumption (OC) calorimetry were in good agreement with those measured by stoichiometric thermochemistry (TC) theory. However, Huang et al. [18] demonstrated that the results of TC differed from those of cone calorimeter, especially for electrolytes containing DEC. Thus, in order to explore the validity of HRR data obtained by cone calorimeter based on OC calorimetry, TC theory was also applied to calculate the theoretical HRR, and the results from two methods were compared.

In this work, two kinds of LIB electrolytes containing carbonate solvents with different lithium salt LiPF6 or LiPF4 were provided by Tianjin Jinniu Co. Ltd. The mass ratio of carbonate solvent EC/PC/EMC was 25:25:50. The physical and chemical properties of relevant materials and samples were listed in Table 1 and Table 2 , respectively.

The cone calorimeter used in this paper was produced by Fire Testing Technology Co., Ltd (FTT, from UK). As shown in Fig. 1 , a typical cone calorimeter includes the following parts: Weighing system, mainly including sample support and high-precision electronic balance; Heat source system, mainly consisting of conical radiant heater, combustion chamber, and related calibration equipment such as radiant heat flow meter; Ventilation system, including fan, smoke hood, etc.; Flue gas analysis system mainly includes multi-parameter gas analyzer and smoke density measurement device; Data acquisition and analysis system, mainly comprising National Instruments (NI) data acquisition instrument and related supporting software.

Flame temperature was measured using K-type thermocouples and a 7018-type temperature acquisition module. The locations of thermocouples, HF detector, and radiant heat flowmeter in Lijiang (76.11 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa) are shown in Fig. 2 . A total of 7 thermocouples were used, and their positions were T1 to T7 as illustrated in Fig. 2. However, due to the space limitation above the cone calorimeter, only one thermocouple was arranged in Hefei (100.09 kPa), at position T1, as presented in Fig. 1.

Based on the OC calorimetry (the basic principle could be expressed as Equation (1) ), the HRR in the combustion process of the sample was calculated, which is a significant parameter to comprehensively reflect the combustion performance of the material.
(1)
q ˙
( t )
=
(
Δ
h c
/
r 0
)
( 1.10 )
C
Δ p
T e
⋅
X
O 2
0
−
X
O 2
1.105 − 1.5
X
O 2
where.
q ˙
( t )
is the HRR of the materials;
( Δ
h c
/
r 0
)
is an empirical value. According to a large number of experimental data, for most substances, for every 1 kg of oxygen consumed during combustion, about 13.1 × 103 kJ heat would be released;
C is the calibration coefficient of orifice flowmeter;
Δ p
is the pressure difference of orifice flowmeter;
T e
is the absolute temperature of the gas at the orifice meter;
X
O 2
0
is the oxygen concentration in the environment measured by the oxygen analyzer during the initial 1-min baseline test;
X
O 2
is the oxygen concentration measured by the oxygen analyzer in the exhaust pipe.
In this paper, a pallet electronic balance (type: Shimadzu UW/UX series) was used for mass measurement. The measurement accuracy is 0.01 g and the maximum range is up to 6.4 kg.

There are many application scenarios of LIBs in low pressure environment, especially in plateau area. At present, there are few studies on the safety and combustion characteristics of electrolyte in low-pressure environment. Therefore, in this paper, two types of electrolytes were selected to conduct pool fire combustion experiments in Hefei (100.09 kPa), Lijiang (76.11 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa). Among them, HRR data were detected in Hefei (100.09 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa). However, due to equipment limitations, HRR could not be obtained in Lijiang (76.11 kPa), so which would only be estimated based on mass loss.

In the experiments, a circular oil pan with a diameter of 10 cm was applied, and 30 mL electrolyte was used each time. In order to ensure the consistency of experimental conditions in the three sites, there was no external radiant heat flow in this paper, and the ignition source burning torch was used to ignite the electrolyte directly.

Fig. 3 illustrates the whole combustion process of electrolyte pool fire in Hefei (100.09 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa). During combustion, the electrolyte evaporated and combustible gases rose under the heat flow, entraining air to complete the combustion process. Although less air was entrained at low pressure, longer entrainer time was required to complete the combustion process, resulting in thinner, taller and longer flame shapes. Therefore, flame height is negatively correlated with pressure. The combustion process could be divided into four stages: ignition, stable combustion stage Ⅰ, stable combustion stage Ⅱ and extinction. Among them, the flame height in the stable combustion stage Ⅰ was significantly higher than that in the stable combustion stage Ⅱ, which corresponded to the two peaks of HRR that would be described in detail in a later section. In this study, the method to determine the stable combustion stage was to observe the experimental video, firstly roughly selecting the stage, and then fit the mass loss rate (MLR) curve to obtain the accurate stable combustion stage. There were two main differences between the two electrolytes that containing LiPF6 and LiBF4, respectively. In the first place, the burning degree of electrolyte with LiPF6 was higher, since which burned more intensely, and the blue flame accounted for a smaller percentage. When the ambient pressure dropped, there were more blue areas in the flame, because lower pressure resulted in less carbon black (CB) production and more blue-radiating polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) generation. In detail, in Fig. 3 there is an obvious phenomenon that with the decrease of pressure the flame brightness became low, and the flame color changed gradually from yellow to blue, which was mainly related to carbon smoke particle volume fraction. The decline of the pressure directly affects the air density. While the density changed, the most direct impact was reducing CB production of fire. The pressure is proportional to the quadratic value of the soot absorption coefficient. In general, CB radiation emits yellow light, which makes the flame more yellow and bright under normal pressure. However, under low pressure, oxygen flow driven by buoyancy was weakened, which hindering the generation of CB. The main products were cyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (AHs), and whose radiation showed light blue. So, with the decrease of pressure, the blue flame area increased. Secondly, as for the electrolyte with LiBF4, the flame color changed from yellow to green completely with the progress of combustion reaction, while the existence of green flame was due to the flame reaction of boron element. Electrolyte is complex mixtures composed of a variety of organic carbonate and lithium salt; among them lithium salt is non-combustible. Under normal circumstances, the electrolyte pool fire presented flame after ignition. When the combustibles gradually exhausted, the flame color would change from yellow to colorless, and yellow flame would largely cover green one, until late combustion flame reaction of boron elements would appear.

Fig. 4 (a) presents curves of center line temperature versus thermocouples position heights for the two electrolytes pool fire in the stable combustion stage in Lijiang (76.11 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa), and Fig. 4 (b) indicates the average flame temperature of two samples at T1 during the stable combustion stages under different pressures. From Fig. 4 (a), the centerline temperature of electrolyte pool fire decreased with the increase of measured position height. From the effect of pressure on temperature, the average temperature detected by T1 to T7 in Lhasa (65.23 kPa) declined with height from 0.04 m to 0.16 m faster than that in Lijiang (76.11 kPa), while the decrease rate of plume between 0.20 m and 0.30 m was slower, which mainly because the flame height in Lhasa (65.23 kPa) was slightly higher than that in Lijiang (76.11 kPa). During combustion processing, electrolytes would be vaporized, and the combustible gases rose under heat flow, entraining air to complete the combustion process. Although less air was entrained at low pressure, longer entraining time was required to complete the combustion process, resulting in thinner, taller and longer flame shapes [19]. Moreover, from 0.04 m to 0.16 m above the liquid level, a thinner flame meant lower energy, while a higher flame temperature resulted in a more obvious outward radiative cooling, so the temperature dropped faster in Lhasa (65.23 kPa) [20]. In Fig. 4 (b), the lower the pressure, the lower the air density, the lower the buoyancy, the lower the plume rise rate, the lower the plume entrainment rate, resulting in the higher temperature of the plume.

In order to further analyze the plume temperature of electrolyte pool fire, the characteristic length z* was introduced to describe the size of fire source, which is defined as Equation (2) [19],
(2)
z ∗
=
(
Q
ρ ∞
c p
T ∞
g
)
2 5
where Q is HRR,
ρ ∞
，
c p
，
T ∞
and g are density, air specific heat capacity, ambient temperature and gravitational acceleration, respectively. The HRR in Lijiang (76.11 kPa) can be calculated from MLR and heat of combustion, while what in Lhasa (65.23 kPa) could be obtained directly with the cone calorimeter. According to the experimental results, z/z* was set as the X-axis and flame temperature was set as Y-axis, which were both influenced by pressure, and the fitting results were taken into consideration, to evaluate whether the flame temperature of electrolyte pool fire conformed to the three-zone distribution proposed by McCaffrey model, as shown in Fig. 5 .

According to McCaffrey's fire plume temperature distribution model, the slope of the center line temperature in the intermittent plume region should be between the flame region and the plume region for axisymmetric ignition sources with hydride combustion condition, showing a ternary temperature distribution. However, as for electrolytes, the boundary between the intermittent zone and the plume zone was not well defined, making it difficult to accurately distinguish between the two zones. Therefore, the flame plume of electrolyte pool fire could be divided into two regions as flame zone and plume zone, which was consistent with previous research [21]. According to McCaffrey's model, the slope in the plume area should be −5/3, while that of electrolyte pool fire was −1.28, indicating that the center line temperature of the electrolyte pool fire dropped faster.

Fig. 6 illustrates the mass and MLR curves of the electrolytes with LiPF6 and LiPF4, respectively in Hefei (100.09 kPa), Lijiang (76.11 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa). Overall, the mass was steadily declining along with time, but there was slightly fluctuation for mass at a partial time point under the influence of test environment pressure and air flow. In order to better reflect the change trend of mass over time, in this article, in the first place, the curves of mass versus time were smoothened, then taken the derivative, finally the minus were picked to obtain the MLR of electrolyte pool fire. The MLR of samples decreased with the reduction of pressure, which was mainly due to the effect from per unit volume of oxygen concentration which also declined with the descending of pressure, and the heat feedback of flame on the smoldering surface. The vapor concentration near the fuel surface was quite high, where the oxygen concentration was close to zero, then the oxygen concentration gradient between air and flame reduced, which led to the decrease of oxygen diffusion rate, gas combustion intensity and MLR.

By comparing the MLR of the two electrolytes, which of that with LiPF6 was higher than that with LiBF4. According to the study of Ping et al. [22], the addition of lithium salts (both LiPF6 and LiBF4) would increase the activity and significantly reduce the thermal stability of the electrolyte. While, compared with LiBF4, the addition of LiPF6 had a greater effect on the thermal stability of electrolyte.

By fitting the experimental data, MLR and pressure showed an exponential relationship, as shown in Fig. 7 . Consistent with the results of previous study [21], as shown in Equation (3) , the exponential coefficients of the two electrolytes were 1.05 and 1.09, respectively. According to the research results of Wieser et al. [23], the exponential coefficients β of diesel, gasoline, methanol and n-heptane pool fires (the diameter of oil pan D = 10 cm) were 1, 0.9, 0.7 and 1.3, respectively. By comparison, the exponential coefficients declined with the decrease of the carbon content in fuel, which mainly because if the fuel contained less carbon, then the less CB would be generated. Relatively, the more fully the fuel was burned, and the smaller the difference of MLR under various pressure environment, therefore the smaller the exponential coefficient. Furthermore, the exponential coefficient of electrolyte was close to that of diesel oil. Generally, this coefficient would rise with the increase of the carbon atoms mass percentage. Through calculation, the mass percentage of carbon atoms in methanol is 0.4375, and what in electrolyte is 0.4507, which is slightly higher than methanol. Therefore, the results of this paper are consistent with previous research [21].
(3)
m ˙
″
∝
A ⋅
P β

HRR is the heat released by fuel combustion per unit time, which is one of the most important parameters to measure the fire risk, and the HRR of electrolyte also directly determines whether the adjacent LIB could be ignited. Fig. 8 indicates the HRR variation curves of two electrolytes pool fires in Hefei (100.09 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa). From the figure, the obtained HRR curves of these carbonate solution mixtures showed a significantly different trend from those of other typical pure flammable substances using a cone calorimeter, because which presented more than one peak. For the tested ternary mixture system, the HRR curve indicated a large peak and a small peak, and the trend could be explained as: multiple peaks in the HRR curves represented the contribution of each individual component in these mixtures to the overall HRR. As the typical cyclic carbonates, EC and PC have relatively high flash point values, making it more difficult for them to form combustible gas mixtures with air than linear carbonates like EMC. In addition, the enthalpy of evaporation for EC and PC is 0.62 kJ/g and 0.55 kJ/g, respectively, which is almost double that of linear EMC (0.34 kJ/g). As a result, the first peak of HRR (pHRR) was contributed by EMC, and the second pHRR was contributed by both the less active EC and PC during the second stage of combustion as the exothermic reaction continued.

From the figure that there is an obvious difference between the pHRR in Hefei (100.09 kPa) and Lhasa (65.23 kPa). Under the condition of low pressure, the pHRR in Lhasa (65.23 kPa) was 69.1% of that in Hefei (100.09 kPa). However, it is worth noting that the THR in Lhasa (65.23 kPa) was not much lower than that in Hefei (100.09 kPa), only with a decrease of about 7.5%–11.5% (from LiBF4 to LiPF6). Due to reduced combustion efficiency, electrolyte pool fires emitted less heat at high altitudes, with lower pHRR and THR, which were consistent with previous studies [15,16]. Besides, according to the research of Chen et al. and Cao et al. [31–37], with the decrease of environmental pressure, the peak value of HRR would be greatly reduced and the time to reach the peak would also be shortened. Therefore, with the decrease of pressure, the two peaks would be combined into one, presenting only one peak.

For the two different lithium salts, the pHRR of the electrolyte with LiBF4 was higher than that with LiPF6 at both atmospheric and low pressures. From the perspective of the THR, due to the small molecular weight (MW) of LiBF4, while the molar concentration of added lithium salts was the same, as for the electrolyte mixture with the same volume of 30 mL, there would be a bit more combustible carbonate solution for electrolyte with lower MW lithium salt. So, the THR of electrolyte with LiBF4 was larger. Moreover, the conductivity of electrolyte with LiBF4 was lower. Therefore, the addition of LiBF4 would not effectively improve the safety of the electrolyte from the above aspects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The comparison of two heat release rate (HRR) calculation methods
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the reliability of the HRR obtained by the cone calorimeter, another method is used in this paper to calculate the HRR. In fact, prior to the development of OC calorimetry, HRR was calculated using a chemical calorimetry based on the production of CO and CO2. However, the application of the TC theory method became gradually narrow with the development of OC calorimetry, which had the great advantage of being able to calculate the HRR without knowing the chemical composition of the fuel. Nevertheless, if the chemical composition of the fuel is known, TC theory would be a very effective method to calculate the HRR of the material. Besides, it has the advantage of not measuring the mass flow rate (MFR) of combustion products in the exhaust pipe.

During combustion processing, the chemical energy of fuel would be converted to heat energy and released. According to Hess's Law and Lavoisier-Laplace's Law [25,26], heat release in a chemical reaction could be determined by calculating the difference in enthalpy of formation between the reactants and the products as follows Equation (4) ,
(4)
− Q =
∑
i = product
n i
⋅ Δ
H
f , i
− =
∑
j = reactant
n j
⋅ Δ
H
f , i

For a typical combustible, the chemical reaction of combustion can be obtained by the following Equation (5) ,
(5) aCxHyOz+1O2→nCO2+bCO + cH2O
where, the coefficient n represents the amount of produced CO2 when per mole of oxygen consumed, which can be obtained by using the increase in CO2 dividing the decrease in O2 during combustion. The coefficients a, b and c in the equation indicate fuel consumption, CO generation. and H2O production, respectively. The expressions of a, b and c can be deduced by the conservation principle of C, H and O atoms before and after the reaction as Equation (6) below,
(6)
a =
( n - 2 )
/z - x - 0.5 y
b = ax - n
c = a - 0.5 y

In combination with Equation (4) and Equation (6) , the following Equation (7) could be gotten,
(7)
- Q = n·Δ
H
f , c
o 2
+ b·Δ
H
f , c o
+ c·Δ
H
f ,
H 2
O
- a·Δ
H
f ,
C x
H y
O z

Finally, the HRR calculated by TC theory can be expressed as follows Equation (8) after simplification,
(8)
q ˙
=
m ˙
C
O 2
44
·
Q
n · S
where
m ˙
C
O 2
denotes the MFR of CO2 in combustion gas products, which could be achieved directly from the output of the cone calorimeter.

In previous study, some scholars believed that thermal decomposition of LIBs carbonate electrolyte would produce a certain amount of CO2 [22]. Thus, not all CO2 generation would come from combustion, so the TC theory used for HRR calculation might have some bias.

Fig. 9 shows the comparison of experimental data of HRR measured from a cone calorimeter based on the OC calorimetry with theoretical values of that calculated by the TC theory. As can be seen from the figure, the error of the two results was above 15% (16.6% for electrolyte with LiPF6, and the other was 16.8%). According to the study from Takeuchi et al. [29], the following reaction Equation (9) would occur between EMC, DEC, and DMC:
(9) 2EMC↔DEC + DMC

Based on the research of Kawamura et al. [10], for electrolytes containing DEC, the following chemical reactions Equation (10) to Equation (14) would occur:
(10) C2H5OCOOC2H5+PH5→C2H5OCOOPH4+HF + CH2 CH2
(11) C2H5OCOOC2H5+PH5→C2H5OCOOPH4+HF + C2H5F
(12) C2H5OCOOPH4→PF3O + CO2+HF + C2H4
(13) C2H5OCOOPH4→PF3O + CO2+C2H5F
(14) C2H5OCOOPH4+HF→PF4OH + CO2+C2H5F

As a result, 2 mol of EMC (containing in the samples) could generate 1 mol of DEC, which could be decomposed itself to produce 1 mol of CO2. Thus, not all the CO2 production was from the complete combustion of electrolyte, which was not corresponding to the hypothesis of TC theory, so the reliability of HRR results calculated by TC was affected.

The smoke produced by combustion directly affects evacuation, so it is necessary to study the smoke release rate. CO2 and CO are the main components of smoke, and the molar concentration ratio of which is proportional to the toxicity risk of fire. From Fig. 10 , after ignition, the release rate of CO2 increased, and the trend of CO2 concentration curve and that of HRR was basically the same, with two peaks, indicating that the generation of CO2 was closely related to the pyrolysis of volatile gas. High flame temperature could promote the oxidation of volatile gas and produce more CO2. The production of CO was related not only to incomplete combustion, but also to the relative evaporation of organic solvents in the electrolyte. EMC had been consumed in the initial stage of combustion, and the remaining combustion process was dominated by EC and PC, whose heat of vaporization is much higher than that of EMC. Therefore, the evaporation process of EC and PC required more heat, which led to the reduction of heat available for the oxidation reaction of volatile gas. In this case, the CO release rate increased. At the late combustion stage, the molar concentration ratio of CO and CO2 rose, showing relatively high toxicity.

In this paper, the combustion characteristics of electrolyte solution with a solvent of EC/PC/EMC (25/25/50, wt/wt/wt) and solute lithium salt LiPF6 or LiBF4 were analyzed under different ambient pressures, and the fire risk of the two electrolytes was evaluated.
(1)
There was a positive exponential relationship between the average MLR and pressure in the stable combustion stage of electrolyte pool fire.
(2)
Under high altitude environment, due to the decrease of combustion efficiency, less heat was released from the electrolyte pool fire. In the meanwhile, the peak and average values of HRR decreased significantly, and the burning duration increased remarkably with the descending of pressure.
(3)
The pHRR of electrolyte with LiBF4 was higher than that of LiPF6 at both atmospheric and low pressures. In terms of exothermic heat, LiBF4 electrolyte had a large THR due to its small MW, so adding LiBF4 cannot effectively improve the safety of the electrolyte.
(4)
Comparing the two HRR calculation methods, TC theory was not suitable for studying the HRR of electrolyte containing EMC.

Although the results in this paper showed that using LiBF4 as the only lithium salt could not obviously improve the safety of the electrolyte, the thermal stability of LiBF4 is relatively excellent, so the follow-up study will be carried out on the synergistic effect of LiBF4 and other lithium salts or additives on the fire hazard of the electrolyte, so as to obtain the electrolyte system with high safety.

This study has been sponsored by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No: 51906238) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 20210302123017). The authors gratefully acknowledge these supports.

Shibiao Qiao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xiaokai Meng: Writing – review & editing. Weiguo Cao: Software, Visualization. Shuo Yu: Investigation, Resources. Changcheng Liu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Que Huang: Methodology, Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, Q. S.; Jiang, L. H.; Yu, Y.; Sun, J. H., Progress of enhancing the safety of lithium ion battery from the electrolyte aspect. Nano Energy 2019, 55, 93-114.
